title.emphasis: Part I: The Architecture of First Amendment Jurisprudence
A. Introduction: The Five Freedoms and the Doctrine of Incorporation

section.para: The First Amendment to the United States Constitution stands as a foundational pillar of American democracy, articulating five core freedoms: "Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the Government for a redress of grievances".1 These liberties—religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition—form the bedrock of public discourse and individual autonomy. However, the amendment's opening words, "Congress shall make no law," historically limited its application to the federal government alone.2 For the first century and a half of the nation's history, states were not bound by its constraints.

This legal reality was fundamentally altered by the adoption of the Fourteenth Amendment after the Civil War, specifically its Due Process Clause, which declares that no state shall "deprive any person of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law." Through a gradual and transformative legal process known as "selective incorporation," the Supreme Court began to rule that certain fundamental rights enumerated in the Bill of Rights were essential components of "liberty" and were therefore protected from state infringement by the Fourteenth Amendment. This doctrine is the essential legal mechanism that makes the entire body of modern First Amendment law possible, transforming a federal constraint into a national charter of freedom. The following cases represent the pivotal moments in which the Court constructed this constitutional architecture.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Gitlow v. New York **, 268 U.S. 652 (1925)**
section.para: This case marks the genesis of modern First Amendment law. Benjamin Gitlow, a Socialist, was convicted under New York's Criminal Anarchy Law for publishing a "Left Wing Manifesto".4 While the Supreme Court upheld his conviction, finding that the manifesto advocated the violent overthrow of the government, the decision's lasting impact came from a crucial passage. The Court declared, "For present purposes we may and do assume that freedom of speech and of the press—which are protected by the First Amendment from abridgment by Congress—are among the fundamental personal rights and 'liberties' protected by the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment from impairment by the States".5 This "assumption" was revolutionary. By incorporating the freedoms of speech and press,
Gitlow established the principle that the First Amendment's protections extend beyond the federal government, setting the stage for the application of all its clauses to state and local governments.5

section.title:
Case Analysis:
De Jonge v. Oregon **, 299 U.S. 353 (1937)**
section.para: Dirk De Jonge was convicted under Oregon's criminal syndicalism statute for assisting in the conduct of a public meeting held by the Communist Party.7 The meeting itself was peaceful and orderly, with the discussion focused on protesting police actions.7 The Supreme Court unanimously reversed the conviction, holding that the freedom of assembly is a right cognate to those of free speech and free press and is likewise protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.10 Chief Justice Charles Evans Hughes wrote that "peaceable assembly for lawful discussion cannot be made a crime".10 The ruling established that the right of assembly could not be denied simply because of the auspices under which a meeting was held; the nature of the assembly itself was determinative.9 This decision incorporated the right of peaceable assembly, making it applicable to the states.6

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Cantwell v. Connecticut **, 310 U.S. 296 (1940)**
section.para: Newton Cantwell and his sons, Jehovah's Witnesses, were convicted of soliciting for religious causes without a license and for inciting a breach of the peace in a heavily Catholic neighborhood.11 The Supreme Court unanimously overturned their convictions, explicitly incorporating the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment to apply to the states.11 The Court found that the licensing statute gave a state official the power to determine what was or was not a "religious cause," which amounted to an unconstitutional prior restraint on the free exercise of religion.13 While acknowledging that the state could regulate the time, place, and manner of solicitation, it could not vest such broad discretion in a government official as to create a system of religious censorship.11

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Everson v. Board of Education **, 330 U.S. 1 (1947)**
section.para: This case was the first in which the Supreme Court explicitly applied the Establishment Clause to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.15 The case concerned a New Jersey law that authorized reimbursement to parents for money spent on transporting their children to school on public buses, including to private and parochial schools.15 A taxpayer challenged the reimbursements for parents of parochial school students as a violation of the Establishment Clause. In a 5-4 decision, the Court upheld the law, reasoning that the reimbursements were a general public welfare benefit provided to all students, regardless of religion, and were therefore separate from the religious function of the schools.16 More importantly, Justice Hugo Black's majority opinion famously invoked Thomas Jefferson's metaphor, declaring that the Establishment Clause was intended to erect "a wall of separation between Church and State".15 This articulation, though not preventing the state action in this specific case, established the "wall of separation" as a central principle in the Court's subsequent Establishment Clause jurisprudence.
